preamble: The complete loss or elision of the voiceless bilabial plosive /p/, a characteristic sound change in the history of Celtic languages and various other lineages.

examples.note: Latvian dropped word-final stops including *-p. Final consonant loss is a distinctive Latvian innovation compared to conservative Lithuanian which often retains final consonants.

examples.to: mempergi [but base p deleted in most stems]
examples.note: Indonesian meN- prefix: initial /p/ is deleted in many bases, with the nasal assimilating to [m]. E.g., 'me- + pukul' → 'memukul' (to hit), underlying /p/ deleted.
Complete loss of PIE *p in Old Irish.

Trans-New Guinea languages characteristically delete word-final obstruents. Proto-Trans-New Guinea is reconstructed with CVC syllables possible only word-finally, but daughter languages such as Kalam systematically delete final stops including *-p, resulting in predominantly open (CV) syllable structure. This coda-stop deletion is widespread across the TNG family.

Enga (Trans-New Guinea, Enga Province, Papua New Guinea) deletes word-final obstruents including /p/. Final vowel devoicing rather than consonant retention is characteristic of Enga phonology, indicating that the open-syllable preference typical of Trans-New Guinea languages is maintained by deleting final stops.

sources: Lewis, H., & Pedersen, H. (1937). A Concise Comparative Celtic Grammar.
